county_metadata.parent_county: Original district (Treaty of Cession with Samoan high chiefs, 1900)
county_metadata.record_losses: Limited western-style civil records exist for this district. Traditional Samoan oral genealogies and church records predate US administration. Navy-era administrative records (1900-1951) were partially transferred to NARA; some remain incomplete.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Eastern District encompasses the eastern portion of Tutuila island, including Pago Pago (the territorial capital and deepwater harbor), Fagatogo (the historic government center), and numerous villages along the harbor and coastline. The district includes the villages of Utulei, Aua, Laulii, and others. Pago Pago harbor is one of the finest natural harbors in the South Pacific and was the primary reason the US established a naval station here in 1899. The district was part of the original Treaty of Cession (1900) between Samoan paramount chiefs and the US Navy. Eastern District has the highest population concentration in American Samoa and is the administrative center of the territory.
county_metadata.research_tips: Eastern District contains Pago Pago, the territorial capital and main harbor of American Samoa. The primary genealogical challenge is that Samoan culture relies heavily on oral genealogy (gafa) maintained by matai (chiefs) and family elders, rather than written records. For documentary research: (1) The American Samoa Historic Preservation Office (ASHPO) in Pago Pago holds archaeological, cultural, and some historical records; (2) The Feleti Barstow Public Library in Pago Pago has a Pacific Islands collection with local histories and government documents; (3) Church records are the single most important documentary source -- the Congregational Christian Church of American Samoa (CCCAS, descended from the London Missionary Society, est. 1830) maintains baptism, marriage, and burial registers at individual churches throughout the district; Catholic parish records (Marist Fathers from 1845) are held at local parishes and the Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago; LDS/Mormon ward records (from 1888) are accessible through FamilySearch; (4) US Navy administration records (1900-1951) are at NARA in Washington, DC and College Park, MD -- Record Group 313 (Naval Operating Forces) and RG 126 (Office of Territories) contain censuses, correspondence, and vital event records compiled by the naval government; (5) Communal land records under Samoan custom are managed through the Land and Titles Division of the High Court of American Samoa in Fagatogo -- these document matai title successions and aiga (extended family) land claims, providing genealogical evidence; (6) FamilySearch has limited but growing collections for American Samoa; the LDS Church has a significant presence in the territory and ward records supplement civil documentation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online land records available for Eastern District. Communal land records under Samoan custom are maintained by the Land and Titles Division of the High Court of American Samoa in Fagatogo. Individual land ownership is rare; approximately 90% of land in American Samoa is communal (held by aiga under matai authority). In-person access only.

source_profiles.source_name: Congregational Christian Church of American Samoa (CCCAS) -- Eastern District Parish Records
source_profiles.repository: Individual CCCAS congregations, Eastern District / CCCAS headquarters, Pago Pago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, and burial registers maintained by individual CCCAS congregations in the Eastern District; the London Missionary Society (LMS) established the first mission in Samoa in 1830 and records from Pago Pago area churches are among the oldest written documents in the territory; records kept in Samoan language
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by congregation; some early registers have been lost to hurricanes and tropical deterioration; mid-20th century forward generally intact

source_profiles.source_name: US Navy Government of American Samoa Administrative Records -- NARA
source_profiles.repository: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), Washington, DC and College Park, MD
source_profiles.coverage_notes: RG 313 (Naval Operating Forces) and RG 126 (Office of Territories) contain records of the US Naval Government of American Samoa including censuses, vital event registers (births, deaths, marriages recorded by the naval government), correspondence, court records, land tenure documents, and reports from Pago Pago naval station. Eastern District as the seat of government has the most extensive coverage
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partially transferred to NARA; some records remain in American Samoa or were lost. Coverage is uneven across decades

source_profiles.source_name: High Court of American Samoa -- Land and Titles Division Records
source_profiles.repository: High Court of American Samoa, Land and Titles Division, Fagatogo
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Matai title succession records and communal land dispute proceedings for Eastern District villages; documents aiga (extended family) relationships, genealogical claims to chiefly titles, and land tenure under Samoan custom; essential for documenting family structure and lineage in Samoan culture
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records from the formal court system begin with US administration in 1900; earlier disputes resolved through traditional fono (council) proceedings were not systematically documented in writing

source_profiles.source_name: American Samoa Census Records -- Naval Government and US Census Bureau
source_profiles.repository: NARA / US Census Bureau / American Samoa Government
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Naval government conducted periodic censuses of American Samoa from 1900; US Census Bureau began including American Samoa in decennial censuses in 1920. Eastern District (including Pago Pago) has the most detailed enumeration. Village-level population data available

source_profiles.source_name: Catholic Parish Records -- Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago, Eastern District
source_profiles.repository: Catholic parishes, Eastern District / Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, and burial registers from Catholic parishes in the Eastern District; Marist Fathers established the Catholic mission in Samoa in 1845; parish records document families who are members of the Catholic community; kept in Samoan and some in Latin/French/English
source_profiles.survival_notes: Variable survival; some early records damaged by tropical climate

source_profiles.source_name: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS) Ward Records -- Eastern District
source_profiles.repository: LDS ward houses, Eastern District / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: LDS missionaries arrived in Samoa in 1888; ward records include membership records, baptisms, marriages, family group sheets, and patriarchal blessings. The LDS Church has a significant presence in American Samoa. FamilySearch has indexed some Samoan records

county_metadata.parent_county: Original district (Deed of Cession of Manu'a, 1904)
county_metadata.record_losses: Manu'a's cession to the US occurred separately in 1904. Records from the Manu'a island group are extremely sparse; most documentation is in church records and oral traditions. Margaret Mead's 1925-1926 fieldwork on Ta'u produced ethnographic records now at the Library of Congress.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Manu'a District comprises three islands: Ta'u (the largest and district seat), Ofu, and Olosega. The district is located approximately 60 miles east of Tutuila. Manu'a holds extraordinary cultural significance as the traditional seat of the Tui Manu'a, one of the highest paramount chief titles in Polynesian history, with legendary origins tracing to the god Tagaloa. The Manu'a islands ceded separately to the US in 1904 under a Deed of Cession signed by Tui Manu'a Elisara. The National Park of American Samoa has units on Ta'u and Ofu. The islands maintain strong traditional Samoan culture and governance. Population has declined in modern decades as residents move to Tutuila or Hawaii.
county_metadata.research_tips: Manu'a District comprises the islands of Ta'u, Ofu, and Olosega, located about 60 miles east of Tutuila. Manu'a has deep cultural significance as the legendary seat of the Tui Manu'a (paramount chief of Manu'a), one of the highest traditional titles in all of Polynesia. The Manu'a islands ceded separately to the US in 1904 under the Deed of Cession signed by Tui Manu'a Elisara. Genealogical research here is extremely reliant on oral traditions: (1) The gafa (oral genealogy) of Manu'a families are among the most carefully preserved in Samoa, as the Tui Manu'a lineage traces back thousands of years in Polynesian tradition -- consult matai elders and the American Samoa Historic Preservation Office; (2) Church records from CCCAS congregations on Ta'u, Ofu, and Olosega are the primary written sources -- the LMS established missions in Manu'a in the 1830s-1840s; (3) Margaret Mead's ethnographic fieldwork on Ta'u (1925-1926) produced detailed observations of Samoan family structure, published in 'Coming of Age in Samoa' -- her field notes and correspondence are at the Library of Congress; (4) US Navy records at NARA (RG 313, RG 126) include Manu'a-specific administrative records from the naval government period; (5) The remoteness of Manu'a means that documentary sources are thin -- researchers should prioritize oral history interviews with family elders, church pastors (faifeau), and village councils (fono). The Manu'a islands have maintained traditional Samoan culture more intact than Tutuila, making oral genealogy particularly reliable here.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online land records available for Manu'a District. Communal land records are maintained by the Land and Titles Division of the High Court in Fagatogo. Manu'a land tenure is governed by Samoan custom with matai authority over aiga lands. In-person access only.

source_profiles.source_name: Congregational Christian Church of American Samoa (CCCAS) -- Manu'a District Parish Records
source_profiles.repository: CCCAS congregations on Ta'u, Ofu, and Olosega
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, and burial registers from CCCAS congregations on the Manu'a islands; LMS missionaries reached Manu'a in the 1830s-1840s; Ta'u village churches have the oldest records. Records kept in Samoan language
source_profiles.survival_notes: Thin survival; remoteness and tropical conditions have caused losses; some registers preserved by individual pastors (faifeau)

source_profiles.source_name: High Court of American Samoa -- Land and Titles Division, Manu'a District Records
source_profiles.repository: High Court of American Samoa, Land and Titles Division, Fagatogo (with Manu'a cases)
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Matai title succession and communal land records for Manu'a villages; the Tui Manu'a title (one of the highest in Polynesian tradition) and its genealogical documentation is of particular importance; cases heard in Fagatogo with Manu'a-specific proceedings
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records begin formally in 1904 (Deed of Cession); pre-cession disputes governed by traditional Samoan custom without systematic written records

source_profiles.source_name: US Navy Government Records -- Manu'a District Administrative Files, NARA
source_profiles.repository: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), Washington, DC and College Park, MD
source_profiles.coverage_notes: RG 313 and RG 126 at NARA contain Manu'a-specific administrative records including the 1904 Deed of Cession, census data, correspondence with the district governor, and vital event records compiled by the naval administration
source_profiles.survival_notes: Coverage is thinner than for Eastern District due to Manu'a's remoteness

source_profiles.source_name: Ethnographic and Oral History Sources -- Manu'a District
source_profiles.repository: Library of Congress / American Samoa Historic Preservation Office / Bernice Pauahi Bishop Museum, Honolulu
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Margaret Mead's field notes from Ta'u (1925-1926) at the Library of Congress document Samoan family structures and social organization. ASHPO holds oral history recordings and cultural documentation. The Bishop Museum in Honolulu holds Pacific Islands ethnographic collections including Samoan materials. These sources provide genealogical context for Manu'a families

source_profiles.source_name: Catholic Parish Records -- Manu'a District
source_profiles.repository: Catholic parishes on Ta'u / Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Limited Catholic parish records from Manu'a; the Marist mission had a smaller presence on the Manu'a islands than on Tutuila; baptism and marriage registers where available
source_profiles.survival_notes: Very thin; some registers may not survive

county_metadata.parent_county: Original territory (uninhabited; administered as part of American Samoa)
county_metadata.record_losses: Rose Island (Rose Atoll) is an uninhabited coral atoll. No permanent population and no civil records. It is a National Wildlife Refuge. Included in FIPS for completeness.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rose Island (Rose Atoll) is an uninhabited coral atoll approximately 130 miles east-southeast of Ta'u in the Manu'a group. It consists of two small islets (Rose and Sand) and a coral reef enclosing a lagoon. The atoll has no permanent human population and never has in recorded history. It was designated a National Wildlife Refuge in 1973 and is part of the Rose Atoll Marine National Monument (2009). Rose Island is included in FIPS as a district of American Samoa for administrative completeness. No genealogical records or civil administration exist for Rose Island.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rose Island (Rose Atoll) is an uninhabited coral atoll approximately 130 miles east-southeast of Pago Pago. It has no permanent population and therefore no genealogical records. The atoll is a National Wildlife Refuge (designated 1973) and part of the Rose Atoll Marine National Monument (designated 2009). Historical records are limited to: (1) Navigational logs from European and American ships that sighted or visited the atoll (18th-19th centuries); (2) US Navy administrative records noting Rose Island as part of American Samoa (NARA RG 313); (3) Scientific expedition records from biological surveys. This district entry exists for FIPS completeness only. No genealogical research is applicable to Rose Island.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Not applicable. Rose Atoll is an uninhabited National Wildlife Refuge with no land records, property ownership, or civil administration.

source_profiles.source_name: US Navy and USFWS Administrative Records -- Rose Atoll
source_profiles.repository: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) / US Fish and Wildlife Service
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Rose Atoll is an uninhabited National Wildlife Refuge. Administrative records consist of naval survey reports, wildlife refuge establishment documents (1973), and Rose Atoll Marine National Monument designation records (2009). No genealogical records exist for this uninhabited atoll
source_profiles.survival_notes: Not applicable -- no population

source_profiles.source_name: Scientific and Navigational Records -- Rose Atoll
source_profiles.repository: Smithsonian Institution / NARA / Various naval archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical navigational logs from ships that sighted Rose Atoll (18th-19th centuries); scientific expedition records from biological surveys of the atoll. These records have no genealogical value but document the administrative history of the atoll as part of American Samoa
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered across multiple repositories; no systematic collection exists

source_profiles.source_name: Rose Atoll National Wildlife Refuge Administrative Records
source_profiles.repository: US Fish and Wildlife Service, Pacific Islands Refuges / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Establishment and management records for the Rose Atoll National Wildlife Refuge (1973) and Marine National Monument (2009). Administrative records only; no population or civil records exist
source_profiles.survival_notes: Not applicable -- uninhabited

source_profiles.source_name: American Samoa Census Records -- Rose Island Administrative Entry
source_profiles.repository: US Census Bureau / American Samoa Government
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Rose Island appears in American Samoa census enumerations as an unpopulated area; included for territorial completeness. Population has been recorded as zero in every enumeration

county_metadata.parent_county: Annexed to American Samoa by Act of Congress (1925); previously claimed by the Jennings family under the Guano Islands Act
county_metadata.record_losses: Swains Island has a very small population (historically under 50). The Jennings/Olohega family controlled the island from the mid-1800s as a copra plantation. Records are extremely limited; the island was added to American Samoa in 1925.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Swains Island (Olohega) is a small coral atoll approximately 200 miles north of the Samoan archipelago, closer geographically to Tokelau than to Tutuila. The island was settled in the mid-1800s by Eli Hutchinson Jennings, an American whaler and trader, and his Samoan-Tokelauan wife. The Jennings family operated a copra plantation and controlled the island. Swains was annexed to American Samoa by Act of Congress on March 4, 1925, having been claimed under the Guano Islands Act. Tokelau has disputed this annexation. The island's population has historically been very small -- the entire community is essentially one extended family. Swains has no airstrip; access is by boat only.
county_metadata.research_tips: Swains Island (also known as Olohega in Samoan) is a small coral atoll approximately 200 miles north of Tutuila. Its unique history makes it a special case for genealogical research: (1) The island was settled in the mid-1800s by Eli Hutchinson Jennings, an American, and his Samoan-Tokelauan wife. The Jennings family established a copra (dried coconut) plantation and effectively controlled the island for over a century. Family records of the Jennings/Olohega lineage are the primary genealogical source; (2) Swains was annexed to American Samoa by Act of Congress in 1925, having previously been claimed under the Guano Islands Act. Administrative records from this period are at NARA; (3) The population has historically been very small (under 50, sometimes as few as 2-10 residents), all related to the Jennings family. Census records from US Navy administration and later territorial censuses at NARA document these residents; (4) Tokelauan cultural connections mean that genealogical records may also exist in Tokelau (a New Zealand territory) and in Tokelauan community records in Samoa and New Zealand; (5) Church records (primarily LDS and CCCAS) for the small Swains community supplement family records. The American Samoa Historic Preservation Office may have documentation related to the island's cultural and historical resources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online land records available. Swains Island land was historically claimed by the Jennings family under the Guano Islands Act. The 1925 Act of Congress annexed the island to American Samoa. Land records, if any, are in NARA or private Jennings family papers.

source_profiles.source_name: Swains Island Land and Plantation Records -- Jennings Family
source_profiles.repository: NARA / American Samoa Government records / Private Jennings family records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Eli Hutchinson Jennings claimed Swains Island in the mid-1850s under the Guano Islands Act and established a copra plantation. Land records, plantation accounts, and family documents trace the Jennings/Olohega lineage. The 1925 Act of Congress annexing Swains to American Samoa is a key legal document. Private family records may be held by Jennings descendants in American Samoa and Hawaii
source_profiles.survival_notes: Fragmented; private family records are not in any public repository; some official documents at NARA

source_profiles.source_name: US Navy and Territorial Government Records -- Swains Island
source_profiles.repository: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), Washington, DC and College Park, MD
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Administrative records from the annexation of Swains Island in 1925 and subsequent territorial administration. Includes census data (very small population, all related to the Jennings family), correspondence, and supply records for the island
source_profiles.survival_notes: Limited documentation for this very small community

source_profiles.source_name: American Samoa Census Records -- Swains Island
source_profiles.repository: NARA / US Census Bureau / American Samoa Government
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Swains Island population enumerated in American Samoa censuses from 1925 onward; the entire population (typically under 50, sometimes as few as 2-10 residents) is documented. Census records effectively serve as a family register for the Jennings/Olohega lineage

source_profiles.source_name: Church Records -- Swains Island (LDS and CCCAS)
source_profiles.repository: LDS Church / CCCAS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Limited church records for the small Swains Island community; LDS and CCCAS records document baptisms, marriages, and family relationships for the Jennings/Olohega family members on the island
source_profiles.survival_notes: Very limited; small population means few records

source_profiles.source_name: Tokelauan and Cross-Pacific Family Records -- Swains Island Connections
source_profiles.repository: Archives New Zealand / Tokelau Administration / American Samoa Historic Preservation Office
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Swains Island's Tokelauan cultural connections mean that genealogical records for the Jennings family may also exist in Tokelau (a New Zealand territory). Archives New Zealand and the Tokelau administration hold records of Tokelauan communities. The Jennings family's Samoan-Tokelauan wife's lineage connects to Tokelauan and Samoan family networks
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered across multiple Pacific repositories

county_metadata.parent_county: Original district (Treaty of Cession with Samoan high chiefs, 1900)
county_metadata.record_losses: Limited western-style civil records. Leone is one of the oldest villages in Samoa with deep traditional records. Church records (LMS/Congregational from the 1830s, Catholic from 1845) are the primary documentary sources.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Western District encompasses the western portion of Tutuila island, including the villages of Leone (the district seat and one of the oldest villages in Samoa), Tafuna (site of Pago Pago International Airport), Nu'uuli, Ili'ili, Mapusaga, Pava'ia'i, and other communities along the western coast. Leone was historically one of the most powerful village polities in Tutuila and the site of the first London Missionary Society mission on the island (1830s). The Western District was part of the original Treaty of Cession (1900). Tafuna plain is the largest flat area on Tutuila and has experienced significant suburban growth as the residential population shifts from Pago Pago harbor. The district includes the National Park of American Samoa's Tutuila unit at Vatia.
county_metadata.research_tips: Western District includes Leone, one of the oldest and most culturally significant villages in all of Samoa, and Tafuna, the site of Pago Pago International Airport and increasing suburban development. Genealogical research approaches: (1) Leone is the site of the first London Missionary Society (LMS) mission station in Tutuila (established 1830s) and consequently has some of the oldest church records in American Samoa -- CCCAS Leone church baptism, marriage, and burial registers from the mid-1800s are among the most complete in the territory; (2) The Catholic Church (Marist Fathers) established missions in the Western District in the 1840s-1850s; parish records are held at local churches and the Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago; (3) Traditional oral genealogies (gafa) are especially well-preserved in Western District villages like Leone, A'asu, and Amanave, which have deep connections to Samoan settlement traditions -- consult matai and village historians; (4) The Land and Titles Division of the High Court records matai title successions and communal land disputes for Western District villages -- these are essential genealogical sources documenting family relationships and chiefly lineages; (5) US Navy administration records at NARA (RG 313, RG 126) include Western District census and administrative data; (6) FamilySearch has some indexed records from LDS wards in the Western District; the Mormon presence is significant and LDS ward records from 1888 forward document baptisms, marriages, and family group sheets.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No online land records available for Western District. Communal land records under Samoan custom are maintained by the Land and Titles Division of the High Court of American Samoa in Fagatogo. Individual land ownership is rare; approximately 90% of land in American Samoa is communal (held by aiga under matai authority). In-person access only.

source_profiles.source_name: Congregational Christian Church of American Samoa (CCCAS) -- Western District Parish Records (Leone and Environs)
source_profiles.repository: CCCAS congregations, Western District / CCCAS headquarters
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Leone was the site of the first LMS mission station on Tutuila (1830s), making Western District church records among the oldest in American Samoa. Baptism, marriage, and burial registers from CCCAS congregations in Leone, Tafuna, Nu'uuli, and other villages. Records in Samoan language
source_profiles.survival_notes: Leone registers from the 1830s-1840s are among the most complete early church records in the territory; later records generally intact except for hurricane damage

source_profiles.source_name: High Court of American Samoa -- Land and Titles Division, Western District Records
source_profiles.repository: High Court of American Samoa, Land and Titles Division, Fagatogo
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Matai title succession records and communal land dispute proceedings for Western District villages including Leone, Tafuna, Nu'uuli, and Amanave; documents family relationships and chiefly lineages under Samoan custom
source_profiles.survival_notes: Formal court records from 1900; pre-cession governance through traditional fono (village council) without systematic written records

source_profiles.source_name: US Navy Government Records -- Western District Administrative Files, NARA
source_profiles.repository: National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), Washington, DC and College Park, MD
source_profiles.coverage_notes: RG 313 and RG 126 at NARA contain Western District administrative records from the naval government period including village censuses, correspondence, vital event records, and reports on local governance
source_profiles.survival_notes: Coverage varies; Western District records are less extensive than Eastern District (seat of government)

source_profiles.source_name: Catholic Parish Records -- Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago, Western District
source_profiles.repository: Catholic parishes, Western District / Diocese of Samoa-Pago Pago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, and burial registers from Catholic parishes in the Western District; Marist Fathers established missions in western Tutuila in the 1840s-1850s. Records in Samoan with some Latin/French entries
source_profiles.survival_notes: Variable survival; some registers damaged by tropical conditions and hurricanes

source_profiles.source_name: Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS) Ward Records -- Western District
source_profiles.repository: LDS ward houses, Western District / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: LDS ward records for Western District congregations including membership records, baptisms, marriages, and family group sheets; the LDS Church has a particularly strong presence in the Tafuna and Nu'uuli areas. FamilySearch may have indexed records

source_profiles.source_name: Western District Village Cemetery Records
source_profiles.repository: American Samoa Historic Preservation Office / Individual village councils
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Village cemeteries in the Western District include family burial grounds maintained by aiga (extended families); tombstone inscriptions and burial locations documented by ASHPO's cultural resource surveys. Many burials are on family land adjacent to homes following Samoan custom
source_profiles.survival_notes: Older graves may lack permanent markers; ASHPO surveys are the best documentation available
